column1: What is an evasion attack in AI security?
column2: An attack that manipulates inference-time inputs to cause a trained model to produce incorrect outputs, bypassing safeguards built into the training pipeline

column1: What is the key difference between training-time and inference-time attacks?
column2: Training-time attacks change what the model learns (like data poisoning), while inference-time attacks only change what the model sees when making predictions

column1: What is transferability in adversarial machine learning?
column2: The property where an adversarial example crafted against a surrogate model can often fool different production models, particularly when architectures or data are similar

column1: How do evasion attacks differ between traditional ML and LLMs?
column2: Traditional ML evasion targets fixed feature representations by manipulating statistics. LLM evasion uses prompt injection to manipulate the model's instruction-following behavior

column1: What is the GoodWords attack?
column2: An adversarial technique that exploits Naive Bayes classifiers by appending carefully selected legitimate words to spam messages to evade detection

column1: What is the "naive" assumption in Naive Bayes that makes it vulnerable?
column2: Conditional independence of features - the assumption that each word contributes independently to probability calculation

column1: Why does the GoodWords attack work mathematically?
column2: Because Naive Bayes uses additive log probabilities, adding good words shifts the probability balance toward ham without the model detecting semantic mismatch

column1: What is the goodness score formula for word selection?
column2: S(w) = P(w
column4: spam) + ε), where words with high scores appear frequently in legitimate messages but rarely in spam

column1: How many good words typically achieve over 90% evasion in the attack?
column2: Between 15 and 30 carefully selected good words achieve evasion rates over 90%

column1: What is the key insight about individual word impact in GoodWords attacks?
column2: No single word dominates - most contribute 2-4% reduction individually. Success requires orchestrated combination of multiple words through accumulation

column1: What are the three phases of black-box GoodWords discovery?
column2: Exploration (40% budget) for broad testing, Exploitation (40%) for refining top candidates, and Combination search (20%) for discovering synergies

column1: What is the epsilon-greedy strategy in black-box attacks?
column2: A selection algorithm that balances exploration (testing new words) with exploitation (using known good words) by randomly exploring a fixed percentage of the time

column1: What is the Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) formula purpose?
column2: UCB = average_reward + c*sqrt(ln(t)/n_w) combines exploitation (average reward) with exploration bonus (uncertainty about rarely-tested words)

column1: Why does the UCB exploration bonus use a square root?
column2: The square root creates a confidence interval reflecting uncertainty about rarely-tested words, which shrinks as testing frequency increases

column1: What does exponential moving average do in score updates?
column2: Smoothly updates word effectiveness beliefs by giving new observations partial weight (e.g. 30%) while retaining historical data (70%), preventing overreaction to outliers

column1: What is synergy in word combinations?
column2: When word pairs or triplets achieve greater spam probability reduction together than the sum of their individual impacts

column1: Why does black-box discovery sometimes find more impactful words than white-box?
column2: White-box measures theoretical ham-association from training data, while black-box measures empirical impact on real spam messages through actual probability shifts

column1: What query budget allocation does the three-phase algorithm use?
column2: 40% exploration, 40% exploitation, 20% combination discovery (e.g., 400-400-200 for 1000 queries)

column1: What is the typical effectiveness comparison between white-box and black-box attacks?
column2: Query-constrained black-box attacks achieve approximately 85-95% of white-box attack effectiveness

column1: What is the decision boundary threshold for successful evasion?
column2: When ham probability exceeds spam probability (> 0.5), the message evades detection regardless of confidence magnitude

column1: Why do good words need minimum frequency thresholds?
column2: Words appearing frequently in legitimate messages signal common communication patterns the classifier learned as ham indicators, while rare words may be flukes or artifacts

column1: What is the purpose of the length filter (3-9 characters) in vocabulary building?
column2: Removes uninformative short tokens (stopwords like "a", "to") and artifacts (URLs, phone numbers) while retaining meaningful conversational words

column1: Why does the attack use log space for probability calculations?
column2: Multiplying thousands of tiny probabilities causes numerical underflow around 10^-308. Logarithms transform multiplication into stable addition

column1: What percentage reduction does adding 10 good words typically achieve?
column2: Approximately 30-40 percentage points reduction in spam probability

column1: What semantic patterns do high-scoring good words share?
column2: Conversational terms (thanks, meeting), temporal references (tomorrow, later), informal contractions (gonna, dont), and polite expressions (please, sorry)

column1: What is the information gain formula in query-based discovery?
column2: I(Q) = H(W) - H(W
column3: Q), measuring reduction in uncertainty about word effectiveness before versus after the query

column1: Why is stratified sampling important in train-test splits?
column2: Maintains the class distribution (e.g., 12.4% spam ratio) across both sets, preventing imbalanced splits that would distort performance metrics

column1: What does the max_features parameter control in CountVectorizer?
column2: Limits vocabulary to a specified number (e.g., 3000) of most important features, preventing overfitting while maintaining expressiveness

column1: What is the purpose of custom token patterns in the vectorizer?
column2: Captures spam-specific features beyond standard words: currency symbols (£$€¥), numbers, and repeated punctuation (!!+, ??+) common in spam

column1: Why remove stop words in feature extraction?
column2: Eliminates non-discriminative terms (the, is, and) that appear equally in both classes and provide no classification value

column1: What does minimal cleaning preserve for spam detection?
column2: Important spam indicators like excessive punctuation, currency references, uppercase words, and symbols that signal spam characteristics

column1: What is the typical class imbalance in spam datasets?
column2: Approximately 13-14% spam messages and 86-87% legitimate messages, mirroring real-world email distributions

column1: Why does heterogeneous vulnerability exist across spam messages?
column2: Messages with fewer spam indicators (near 0.7-0.8 probability) require less augmentation, while those with multiple reinforcing indicators (>0.95) need more words

column1: What is the exploit-explore dilemma in black-box attacks?
column2: Balancing between testing new untested words to discover better options (exploration) versus using known effective words for immediate success (exploitation)

column1: What does the multi-armed bandit framework model?
column2: Each word as a slot machine arm with unknown effectiveness. Players must allocate limited plays to maximize reward when payout probabilities are unknown

column1: What logarithmic regret guarantee does UCB provide?
column2: Cumulative difference from optimal choices grows as O(log T), sublinear with query count T, meaning exploration mistakes remain bounded

column1: Why can't defenders rely on obscuring model internals?
column2: Attackers can empirically discover effective attack vectors through systematic black-box testing, achieving 85-95% of white-box effectiveness

column1: What is the fundamental architectural vulnerability in Naive Bayes?
column2: Features combine additively in log space, creating systematic exploitability regardless of whether attackers know exact probabilities

column1: What makes the 1000-query budget sufficient?
column2: Disciplined allocation across exploration, exploitation, and combination search discovers highly effective words without exhaustive testing

column1: Why does white-box goodness score differ from black-box practical impact?
column2: Goodness measures theoretical ham-association from training. Impact measures actual probability shifts on real spam. High goodness doesn't guarantee high impact

column1: What is the purpose of testing combinations on multiple messages?
column2: Prevents overfitting to single message patterns and improves reliability by identifying combinations that work across diverse spam characteristics

column1: What does the phrase "quantity overwhelms quality" mean in this context?
column2: The classifier cannot penalize semantic mismatch between appended legitimate words and spam content, so accumulated evidence overturns initial spam signal

column1: Why is model persistence with pickle important?
column2: Saves computation time by avoiding retraining, maintains exact vocabulary through transform (not fit_transform), ensuring feature space consistency

column1: What does the 99.22% training vs 98.64% test accuracy indicate?
column2: Minimal gap (0.58%) confirms the model generalizes well without memorizing training data, avoiding overfitting

column1: Why are spam metrics slightly lower than ham metrics?
column2: Class imbalance means fewer spam examples to learn from (12.4% vs 87.6%), reducing model confidence about spam patterns

column1: What is the sigmoid curve pattern in attack effectiveness?
column2: Phase 1 (0-5 words): minimal evasion. Phase 2 (5-15 words): rapid transition from 41% to 96%. Phase 3 (15-20 words): saturation at 100%

column1: What is data poisoning in adversarial machine learning?
column2: A training-time attack that injects or alters training samples so the model internalizes biased patterns

column1: What is label manipulation?
column2: A training-time attack that tampers with annotations so ground truth no longer matches reality

column1: What are Trojan attacks?
column2: Training-time attacks that implant hidden triggers which activate specific behavior when an attacker's pattern appears in the input

column1: What distinguishes inference-time manipulation from training-time attacks?
column2: Inference-time only changes what the model sees during prediction, leaving training and parameters untouched

column1: What is prompt injection in the context of LLMs?
column2: An evasion pattern where adversarial directives are placed in prompts so the model prioritizes them over original system guidance

column1: Why is prompt injection particularly dangerous for LLMs?
column2: The output itself is an action surface (code, queries, markup) that other systems might execute, extending the attack beyond initial response

column1: What is the bag-of-words assumption exploited in spam filters?
column2: The model uses term frequencies for classification, so adding benign tokens changes frequencies while message reads the same to humans

column1: What technique do static malware detectors use that makes them vulnerable?
column2: They use byte patterns and crafted signatures, which can be altered by rearranging sections or perturbing imports without changing program intent

column1: What is the core principle shared by traditional ML and LLM evasion?
column2: Modify only the input seen at prediction time to steer the model's behavior, without changing parameters

column1: Where does leverage come from in traditional ML evasion?
column2: From manipulating structured features and label thresholds by targeting the statistics that feed the classifier

column1: Where does leverage come from in LLM evasion?
column2: From exposing conversational state and instruction following through carefully placed text and context management

column1: What did Lowd and Meek's 2005 paper introduce?
column2: The GoodWords attack technique against statistical spam filters using Naive Bayes classifiers

column1: What is the mathematical foundation targeted by GoodWords?
column2: The classifier's reliance on word frequency distributions and conditional probabilities with feature independence assumptions

column1: What is the key insight of the GoodWords augmentation strategy?
column2: Preserve original spam message intact while augmenting with tokens that shift probability distribution toward legitimate class

column2: To avoid numerical underflow when multiplying many small probabilities

column1: What metaphor explains how the classifier weighs evidence?
column2: A scale weighing evidence for spam versus ham, where each word adds weight to one side or other

column1: What happens to spam probability when good words are appended?
column2: Original spam words contribute same evidence, but added good words shift balance by adding more weight to ham side

column1: What is the mathematical condition for attack success?
column2: log P(ham

column1: What determines when probability reversal occurs?

column1: What is epsilon in the goodness score formula?
column2: A small constant (like 1e-10) added to prevent division by zero when words never appear in spam

column1: Why rank words by goodness scores?
column2: To prioritize candidates with strong discriminative power favoring legitimate class for most efficient attack

column1: What additional constraints should word selection consider?
column2: Avoid words that might trigger rule-based filters or appear suspicious to human reviewers despite high goodness scores

column1: What is the trade-off in choosing number of words to add?
column2: Too few may fail to shift probability sufficiently, too many could appear unnatural or exceed transmission limits

column1: Why can't Naive Bayes penalize semantic mismatch?
column2: It doesn't model word relationships or broader context, only treats each word as independent contribution

column1: What makes learned distributions vulnerable to exploitation?
column2: Training fixes word-class probabilities from historical data, producing static distributions attackers can study systematically

column1: How does Laplace smoothing increase attack susceptibility?
column2: Assigns non-zero probability to unseen/rare words, preventing rejection of unusual combinations and increasing available word range

column1: What four factors create GoodWords attack conditions?
column2: Independence assumption, additive scoring in log space, static learned distributions, and smoothing effects

column1: What is the SMS Spam Collection dataset size?
column2: 5,574 labeled messages from UCI Machine Learning Repository

column1: What does Path.mkdir(exist_ok=True) accomplish?
column2: Creates directory if it doesn't exist without error, making code idempotent and preventing errors on first run

column1: What performance gain does caching provide?
column2: 40× speedup: initial download takes 2-3 seconds, cached reads complete under 50ms

column1: Why extract in-memory rather than to disk?
column2: Avoids filesystem I/O overhead, reducing operation time by half compared to disk write + disk read

column1: What does len(parts) == 2 validation prevent?
column2: Guards against corrupted records from blank lines, partial records, or malformed entries in real-world datasets

column1: What does html_module.unescape() handle?
column2: Converts HTML entities like & to actual characters (&) for messages scraped from web sources

column1: What does Unicode normalization with 'NFKC' mode do?
column2: Converts characters to canonical composed form, e.g., ligature "fi" becomes "fi", preventing encoding variation issues

column1: Why handle whitespace with three separate regex patterns?
column2: Ensures consistent processing: \s+ for spaces/tabs, \n+ for newlines, \r+ for carriage returns across different text sources

column1: Why preserve currency symbols and punctuation in cleaning?
column2: They carry discriminative power: excessive exclamation marks signal urgency, currency symbols indicate monetary offers (spam characteristics)

column1: What does lowercase conversion achieve?
column2: Ensures "FREE" and "free" become same feature, preventing vocabulary duplication

column1: Why remove exact duplicates by label and message?
column2: Eliminates 419 duplicates that could skew training, focusing on unique message patterns

column1: What does stratify=y parameter ensure?
column2: Maintains the 12.4% spam ratio in both train and test sets, preventing imbalanced splits

column1: Why use .values to extract from DataFrame?
column2: Extracts NumPy arrays for more efficient processing by scikit-learn algorithms

column1: What does the 80-20 split provide?
column2: 4,122 training messages for learning patterns, 1,031 testing messages for reliable performance evaluation

column1: Why use transform() instead of fit_transform() when loading saved models?
column2: Uses exact vocabulary from original training, maintaining feature space consistency by ignoring new words

column1: What does max_features=3000 prevent?
column2: Prevents overfitting while maintaining expressiveness by limiting vocabulary to most important features

column1: What token pattern captures in the custom regex?
column2: Standard words (\b\w+\b), currency symbols ([£$€¥]+), numbers (\d+), and repeated punctuation (!!+, \?\?+, \.\.+)

column1: Why does spam detection show 95% precision and 94% recall?
column2: Lower than ham (99%) due to fewer spam examples (12.4%) providing less data for model to learn confident spam patterns

column1: What does feature_log_prob_ attribute contain?
column2: Log P(w
column3: c) for each word w and class c, with index [0] for ham and [1] for spam probabilities

column1: Why store (word, goodness, ham_prob, spam_prob) tuples?
column2: Preserves complete information for validation requiring raw conditional distributions beyond derived goodness scores

column1: What does np.exp(ham_log_probs[i]) recover?
column2: The original probability stored in log space, e.g., exp(-5.3) ≈ 0.005

column1: What linguistic patterns appear in top good words?
column2: Singaporean slang (lor), ellipsis (...), temporal words (later), action verbs (doing, ask), and informal terms (lol, cos)

column1: Why shuffle candidates and messages before testing?
column2: Prevents systematic biases if attack is interrupted or reaches budget limits early

column1: What does augment_message function do?
column2: Appends good words to message with proper spacing, or returns original if no words added

column1: When does predict_proba indicate successful evasion?
column2: When prob[0] > prob[1], meaning ham probability exceeds spam probability (crossing 0.5 threshold)

column1: Why doesn't probability magnitude matter for evasion?
column2: Binary decision only cares which class wins; prob [0.501, 0.499] evades same as [0.99, 0.01]

column1: What progression does the attack show from 0 to 20 words?
column2: 0 words: 6.25% evasion, 5 words: 41%, 10 words: 74%, 15 words: 96%, 20 words: 100%

column1: What does the rapid 5-15 word transition indicate?
column2: Catastrophic failure zone where evasion explodes from 41% to 96%, not gradual degradation

column1: Why use 12×6 inch figure size?
column2: Creates 2:1 aspect ratio giving horizontal progression curve adequate space without vertical compression

column1: What does fill_between with alpha=0.3 emphasize?
column2: Adds translucent area beneath curve emphasizing cumulative nature of attack success

column1: What does the 50% threshold line represent?
column2: Equipoise point where attack becomes more successful than not - filter fails more often than succeeds

column1: What does the 90% threshold line indicate?
column2: Near-complete evasion where only 10% of spam remains caught, representing practical attack success

column1: What does zorder=5 parameter control?
column2: Rendering order ensuring peak scatter point appears above grid lines and fill area

column1: Why test only 50 messages for word impact analysis?
column2: Provides statistical reliability without excessive computation for measuring individual word effects

column1: What does prob_orig - prob_aug quantify?
column2: Impact in absolute terms (percentage points), e.g., 0.95 to 0.85 is 10 percentage point reduction

column1: Why multiply impact by 100 in reporting?
column2: Converts to percentage points for easier interpretation (0.10 becomes 10%)

column1: Why doesn't individual word impact match goodness score?
column2: Goodness measures theoretical ham-association, impact measures actual ability to shift spam-laden messages toward ham

column1: What does horizontal bar chart format provide?
column2: Better readability for word labels compared to vertical bars requiring rotated text

column1: What does uniform grey coloring in impact chart indicate?
column2: All tested words fall below threshold where individual words could single-handedly flip classifications

column1: Why test probability shifts on 8 sample messages?
column2: Enough examples to see patterns without overcrowding visualization

column1: What do grouped bars with 0.15 width allow?
column2: Five groups of bars (0, 5, 10, 20, 30 words) to fit comfortably for each message

column1: What does color progression from MALWARE_RED to AQUAMARINE represent?
column2: Visual transformation from spam (no augmentation) to ham (maximum augmentation)

column1: What does green checkmark on bars indicate?
column2: Successful evasion where spam probability dropped below 0.5 decision threshold

column1: Why does message M7 show extreme vulnerability?
column2: Starts with low spam probability before augmentation, achieving evasion immediately with just 5 words

column1: What explains variance in message vulnerability?
column2: Initial spam probability and specific indicator density - borderline cases resist less than messages with extreme markers

column1: What does monotonic downward trend confirm?
column2: Attack operates through reliable mechanism: every message responds to augmentation by reducing spam probability

column1: Why test on messages 30-50 instead of 0-50?
column2: Provides fresh samples for unbiased evaluation while maintaining some consistency with discovery phase

column1: What does partial overlap in evaluation ensure?
column2: Tests generalization to new examples while avoiding complete dataset shift

column1: What typical black-box progression shows?
column2: 0 words: 5-10% evasion, 10 words: 70-75%, 15 words: 90-95%, 20+ words: 95-100%

column1: Why is black-box progression slightly less steep?
column2: Operates under severe information constraints without access to internal probability tables

column1: What does 85-95% effectiveness comparison reveal?
column2: Query-constrained attacks nearly match full-knowledge attacks, showing broad attack surface exists

column1: What does discovering different but equally effective words prove?
column2: Multiple attack vectors exist - many different good word combinations successfully evade classifier

column1: What does 1000-query sufficiency demonstrate?
column2: Disciplined allocation delivers strong results without exhaustive testing, making attacks practical

column1: Why does obscuring internals provide limited security?
column2: Attackers empirically discover alternative vectors through systematic testing despite missing probability tables

column1: What is the purpose of the /health endpoint?
column2: Confirms service readiness and returns status with service name verification

column1: What does /challenge endpoint return?
column2: Base spam message and constraints including max_added_words and target_label

column1: What does /predict endpoint provide?
column2: Model's label and spam probability for arbitrary text without exposing internals

column1: What does /submit endpoint validate?
column2: Augmented text equals base message plus at most max_added_words using same tokenization as model

column1: What does a successful /submit response include?
column2: Result status, details (label, spam_probability, words_added), and the flag

column1: Why use letter-only tokenization constraint?
column2: Ensures consistency between client word counting and server validation using identical parsing rules

column1: What is the two-phase skills assessment structure?
column2: Phase 1: white-box attack flipping positive to negative. Phase 2: black-box attack flipping negative to positive

column1: What resources does white-box phase provide?
column2: Full model download access for analysis with 30 word maximum per review

column1: What constraints does black-box phase impose?
column2: Limited to API queries only with 40 word maximum per review

column1: What does /model/download endpoint return?
column2: Complete pickle bundle with trained classifier, vectorizer, feature names, and class labels

column1: What does /status endpoint track?
column2: Current progress across both phases showing successful and failed review flips

column1: Why does black-box phase allow 40 words versus white-box 30?
column2: Compensates for information constraints - discovery requires more words without direct probability access

column1: What must be achieved to receive the flag?
column2: Successfully flip all 10 reviews in both phases while respecting word budget constraints
